questions.question: What was the significance of the Partition of India in 1947?
options: The Partition of India was a peaceful process that created two harmonious independent nations in 1947

The Partition of India (August 1947) divided British India into two independent nations: India (majority Hindu) and Pakistan (majority Muslim). The partition caused one of history's largest mass migrations (10-20 million people) and one of the deadliest communal violence episodes (~1 million deaths in Punjab and Bengal). It was drawn hastily by Cyril Radcliffe in six weeks with minimal local consultation. The trauma still shapes India-Pakistan relations
The Partition of India was proposed by Mahatma Gandhi and implemented peacefully; the violence that occurred was exaggerated by the British press
The Partition of India divided the subcontinent into three nations: India, West Pakistan, and East Pakistan (now Bangladesh) in simultaneous 1947 agreements; Bangladesh achieved independence in 1950
questions.answer: The Partition of India (August 1947) divided British India into two independent nations: India (majority Hindu) and Pakistan (majority Muslim). The partition caused one of history's largest mass migrations (10-20 million people) and one of the deadliest communal violence episodes (~1 million deaths in Punjab and Bengal). It was drawn hastily by Cyril Radcliffe in six weeks with minimal local consultation. The trauma still shapes India-Pakistan relations
questions.explanation: Context: Congress Party (mostly Hindu leadership) and Muslim League (Jinnah) could not agree on post-independence governance. Mountbatten (last Viceroy) accelerated independence from June 1948 to August 1947. Cyril Radcliffe — a lawyer who had never been to India — drew the Radcliffe Line in 6 weeks, dividing Punjab and Bengal. He never returned to India. The line split villages, farms, irrigation systems, railroads. Violence: Sikhs, Hindus, and Muslims attacked each other in trains and villages; estimates range 200,000-2 million dead. Bangladesh: East Pakistan (1947-1971), achieved independence after Pakistani military genocide (3 million dead), aided by Indian military intervention — 1971. Kashmir: disputed since 1947, has caused three India-Pakistan wars.
questions.memory_hook: Cyril Radcliffe drew the border that divided India and Pakistan in six weeks. He had never been to India before. He worked from maps and census data. When the border was published (two days after independence, to prevent violence before independence rather than after — a calculation that failed), people in the divided Punjab found out by reading the newspaper whether they were in India or Pakistan. Communities that had lived together for generations had 48 hours to decide whether to flee. 10-20 million people moved. ~1 million were killed. Radcliffe destroyed his papers and never spoke publicly about the partition. He reportedly said he wished he had refused the appointment.

questions.question: What was the Salt March and how did it affect Indian independence?
options: The Salt March was a 1920 protest in which Gandhi marched from Bombay to New Delhi; it ended colonial salt taxes immediately

Gandhi's Salt March (March-April 1930) was a 240-mile walk from Sabarmati Ashram to the Arabian Sea coast at Dandi, where Gandhi illegally made salt to defy the British salt monopoly. The march galvanized the Indian independence movement, resulted in 60,000 arrests including Gandhi, attracted global media coverage, and demonstrated that civil disobedience could effectively challenge colonial power. India achieved independence in 1947
The Salt March was a 1942 British attempt to suppress salt production in Bengal; Gandhi organized armed resistance in response
The Salt March resulted in British concessions: they immediately lowered salt taxes by 50% and released all political prisoners
questions.answer: Gandhi's Salt March (March-April 1930) was a 240-mile walk from Sabarmati Ashram to the Arabian Sea coast at Dandi, where Gandhi illegally made salt to defy the British salt monopoly. The march galvanized the Indian independence movement, resulted in 60,000 arrests including Gandhi, attracted global media coverage, and demonstrated that civil disobedience could effectively challenge colonial power. India achieved independence in 1947
questions.explanation: Salt tax context: Britain's salt laws forbade Indians from producing or selling salt without paying taxes — targeting even the poorest, who used salt to preserve food. Gandhi selected salt deliberately as a symbol accessible to all classes. The 24-day march (March 12 – April 6, 1930): crowds swelled from 79 marchers to thousands. At Dandi, Gandhi picked up salt from the beach. Across India, millions joined salt-making protests. Participants were beaten by police while not resisting; international press covered the beatings. 60,000+ arrested. Gandhi was imprisoned but negotiations followed. The Irwin-Gandhi Pact (1931) suspended civil disobedience; Gandhi attended the London Round Table Conference. The march didn't immediately end British rule but permanently damaged its moral legitimacy.
questions.memory_hook: Gandhi's genius with the Salt March was targeting something the British couldn't easily defend in international press. Taxing the poor's access to salt — a biological necessity — and then beating nonviolent protesters for making it on the beach looked exactly as bad as it was. The American press covered it extensively. The British authorities beat protesters with lathis (metal-tipped bamboo clubs); protesters fell, didn't fight back, and the next group stepped forward to be beaten. Webb Miller of United Press reported it globally. The moral contrast — violent colonizers, nonviolent resisters — was the entire strategic point, and it worked.

questions.question: What was the significance of the Missouri Compromise (1820)?
options: The Missouri Compromise admitted Missouri as a slave state and prohibited slavery in all US territories

The Missouri Compromise (1820) admitted Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state (maintaining Senate balance), and drew a line at 36°30' latitude — slavery permitted south, prohibited north — through the Louisiana Purchase territory. It temporarily settled the sectional crisis over slavery expansion but was repealed by the Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854), triggering the crisis that led to the Civil War
The Missouri Compromise permanently banned slavery in all territories west of the Mississippi River
The Missouri Compromise was drafted by Abraham Lincoln as a young senator; it successfully ended slavery agitation until the Civil War
questions.answer: The Missouri Compromise (1820) admitted Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state (maintaining Senate balance), and drew a line at 36°30' latitude — slavery permitted south, prohibited north — through the Louisiana Purchase territory. It temporarily settled the sectional crisis over slavery expansion but was repealed by the Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854), triggering the crisis that led to the Civil War
questions.explanation: Context: Missouri's application for statehood (1819) triggered crisis because it would upset the 11-11 slave/free state balance in the Senate. Henry Clay engineered the compromise. The 36°30' line became the dividing principle for three decades. Thomas Jefferson called it 'a fire bell in the night' — warning of future crisis. The Dred Scott decision (1857) by Chief Justice Taney declared the Missouri Compromise unconstitutional and ruled that Congress could not prohibit slavery in territories. This ruling outraged the North and helped elect Lincoln (1860). Lincoln's election caused Southern secession and the Civil War (1861). The Missouri Compromise bought 30 years of peace while kicking the slavery question forward.
questions.memory_hook: Thomas Jefferson, in retirement at Monticello with his enslaved household, called the Missouri Compromise 'a fire bell in the night' that awakened him with terror for the Republic. He understood in 1820 exactly what the slavery question would eventually require: war or dissolution. He was 77 years old. He wrote that the Missouri question was 'like a fire bell in the night' and predicted the Union could not survive the contradiction. He died six years later. The Civil War he predicted came 35 years after his death. His terror in 1820 was correct; the compromise he watched just postponed what he feared.

questions.question: What records does Wayne Gretzky hold in NHL hockey and why is he called 'The Great One'?
options: Wayne Gretzky holds the NHL record for career goals (894) but not assists; Bobby Orr holds the career assists record

Wayne Gretzky is the NHL's all-time leader in goals (894), assists (1,963), and points (2,857). His assist total alone exceeds the total points of every other player in NHL history. He played 20 seasons (1979-1999) for Edmonton, Los Angeles, St. Louis, and New York. He is widely considered the greatest player in any team sport relative to his peers. Gretzky led the Edmonton Oilers to four Stanley Cup championships (1984, 1985, 1987, 1988)
Wayne Gretzky holds career points records but is tied with Mario Lemieux for career goals
Wayne Gretzky won 10 consecutive scoring titles but never won the Hart Trophy (MVP); his value was primarily defensive
questions.answer: Wayne Gretzky is the NHL's all-time leader in goals (894), assists (1,963), and points (2,857). His assist total alone exceeds the total points of every other player in NHL history. He played 20 seasons (1979-1999) for Edmonton, Los Angeles, St. Louis, and New York. He is widely considered the greatest player in any team sport relative to his peers. Gretzky led the Edmonton Oilers to four Stanley Cup championships (1984, 1985, 1987, 1988)
questions.explanation: Gretzky stats: 894 goals, 1,963 assists, 2,857 points in 1,487 regular season games. Plus 382 playoff points. His assists alone (1,963) exceed the total career points of any other NHL player in history — meaning if you removed all of his goals, he would still be the all-time points leader on assists alone. Hart Trophy (MVP): 9 times. Art Ross Trophy (scoring title): 10 consecutive times. His dominance was so complete that comparing him to peers is nearly impossible — he was in a different statistical universe. He was traded from Edmonton to Los Angeles in 1988 for $15 million — one of the most controversial trades in Canadian sports history; Canadians were furious.
questions.memory_hook: Wayne Gretzky's assist total alone — 1,963 — exceeds the total career points of every other player in NHL history. Remove every goal he scored and he would still be the all-time points leader. This is the most extreme statistical outlier in professional team sports. The closest analog is Babe Ruth's 1920 season, when he hit more home runs than any other entire team. Gretzky was to hockey what Ruth was to baseball: not just the best, but categorically different from everyone else. He was so dominant so young that the Edmonton Oilers paid him $5,000 salary for his first WHA game — at age 17.

questions.question: What is the significance of the New York Yankees' historic dominance in MLB?
options: The New York Yankees have won 15 World Series championships, the most in MLB history

The New York Yankees have won 27 World Series championships — more than any other franchise in Major League Baseball. Their dynasties include the Ruth/Gehrig era (1920s-30s), the DiMaggio era (1940s-50s), the Mantle era (1950s-60s), and the Jeter/Rivera era (1990s-2000s). The Yankees last won in 2009. The franchise's success derives from New York's large market, enabling high payrolls, and sustained front-office investment
The New York Yankees have won 27 consecutive World Series titles from 1947 to 1973
The New York Yankees have won 27 championships but their most recent dynasty ended with Babe Ruth's trade to the Red Sox in 1919
questions.answer: The New York Yankees have won 27 World Series championships — more than any other franchise in Major League Baseball. Their dynasties include the Ruth/Gehrig era (1920s-30s), the DiMaggio era (1940s-50s), the Mantle era (1950s-60s), and the Jeter/Rivera era (1990s-2000s). The Yankees last won in 2009. The franchise's success derives from New York's large market, enabling high payrolls, and sustained front-office investment
questions.explanation: Yankees championships by era: Babe Ruth/Lou Gehrig (1923-1928: 3 WS), Joe DiMaggio (1936-1953: 9 WS), Mickey Mantle (1949-1964: multiple), Billy Martin/Reggie Jackson (1977-1978: 2 WS), Joe Torre/Derek Jeter (1996-2000: 4 in 5 years), 2009 (last). The 'Curse of the Bambino': Boston Red Sox sold Babe Ruth to Yankees in 1920; Red Sox didn't win another championship until 2004. Yankees-Red Sox rivalry is the most storied in US sports. Aaron Judge broke Roger Maris's American League HR record (61) with 62 HRs in 2022. The Yankees are the most valuable MLB franchise (~$7 billion).
questions.memory_hook: The Boston Red Sox sold Babe Ruth to the Yankees in 1920 for $100,000 — so that Red Sox owner Harry Frazee could fund a Broadway musical. Ruth immediately began winning championships with New York. The Red Sox didn't win another World Series until 2004, 86 years later. The 'Curse of the Bambino' became one of the most famous stories in American sports folklore. When the Red Sox finally broke the curse in 2004 — coming back from 3-0 down in the ALCS against the Yankees — it was the greatest comeback in baseball history and one of the most emotionally charged moments in American sports.

questions.question: Which 1977 album by Fleetwood Mac became one of the best-selling albums ever during a period of intense band turmoil?
options: Tusk
Mirage
Tango in the Night
Rumours
questions.explanation: Rumours (1977) by Fleetwood Mac was recorded while the band's two couples (Lindsey Buckingham/Stevie Nicks, John McVie/Christine McVie) were both going through simultaneous divorces and breakups. The resulting album — with songs openly about the relationships falling apart — became one of the best-selling albums in history (40+ million copies). Songs include 'Go Your Own Way,' 'Dreams,' 'The Chain,' 'Gold Dust Woman.' It won Album of the Year at the 1978 Grammys. 'Dreams' resurged in 2020 when Nathan Apodaca's skateboarding/cranberry juice TikTok video went viral.
questions.memory_hook: Fleetwood Mac recorded Rumours in 1976-77 while Buckingham and Nicks had broken up their romantic relationship but remained musical partners; the McVies had just announced their divorce after 8 years of marriage; Mick Fleetwood was going through a divorce; and drummer Fleetwood had discovered his wife was having an affair with his best friend. All of this was happening simultaneously in a band of five people who had to show up to the same recording sessions. The result — one of the best-selling albums of all time — is essentially a documentary of the emotional wreckage. Every song is about these relationships. The album sold because listeners could feel it was real.

questions.question: Which 1960 Alfred Hitchcock film is considered the first slasher film and features the iconic shower scene?
options: The Birds
Rope
Rear Window
Psycho
questions.explanation: Psycho (1960) directed by Alfred Hitchcock follows Marion Crane (Janet Leigh) who steals money and checks into the Bates Motel, run by the unassuming Norman Bates (Anthony Perkins) and his controlling mother. The shower scene (45 seconds, 70 camera cuts, no explicit nudity but overwhelming psychological terror) is one of cinema's most analyzed sequences. The film violated audience expectations by killing its apparent protagonist in the first third. Hitchcock released it in black and white to reduce the horror of blood; he kept the ending secret and required theaters to refuse entry to latecomers.
questions.memory_hook: Hitchcock made Psycho quickly and cheaply (6 weeks, $800,000) because Paramount refused to finance it — he funded it himself with his TV show profits and shot it with his television crew. Paramount distributed it but wouldn't promote it. The shower scene took 7 days to film. Janet Leigh was so disturbed she never took showers again, only baths. Bernard Herrmann's shrieking string score for the murder was added over Hitchcock's objection — but Hitchcock was so impressed he doubled Herrmann's fee. The film grossed $50 million (60x its budget) and invented the slasher genre. Hitchcock reportedly stood outside theaters and personally refused entry to latecomers.

questions.question: Which novel by F. Scott Fitzgerald, published in 1925, is often called the 'Great American Novel'?
options: This Side of Paradise
Tender Is the Night
The Beautiful and Damned
The Great Gatsby
questions.explanation: The Great Gatsby (1925) by F. Scott Fitzgerald follows Nick Carraway, who observes the obsessive pursuit by Jay Gatsby (a self-made millionaire of mysterious origins) of Daisy Buchanan, Nick's cousin and Gatsby's lost love. Set in Long Island's Gold Coast and New York in the 1920s, the novel critiques the American Dream — the hollow pursuit of wealth and status, the corruption of idealism, and the impossibility of recapturing the past. 'The green light' at the end of Daisy's dock is one of literature's most famous symbols.
questions.memory_hook: The Great Gatsby flopped initially — selling only ~20,000 copies in 1925, far fewer than Fitzgerald's first novel. Fitzgerald died in 1940 believing himself a failure, his reputation spent. The novel's rehabilitation came during WWII, when the Council on Books in Wartime distributed 155,000 copies to soldiers — chosen as a symbol of what America was fighting for. Soldiers came home having read it; a new generation discovered it. By the 1950s it was on school curricula. Fitzgerald never knew his novel would be considered the Great American Novel. He died of a heart attack at 44, drinking heavily, writing screenplays in Hollywood.

questions.question: What is the geographic and ecological significance of the Galapagos Islands?
options: The Galapagos Islands are in the Caribbean Sea; they are significant for coral reef biodiversity and pirate history

The Galapagos Islands (Ecuador, 1,000 km west of the South American mainland) are a volcanic archipelago known for extreme species endemism — animals that evolved in isolation found nowhere else. Darwin's 1835 visit and observation of distinct finch species per island contributed to his theory of natural selection. Today the islands host giant tortoises, marine iguanas (the only ocean-swimming lizard), blue-footed boobies, and flightless cormorants
The Galapagos Islands are significant for oil reserves; Ecuador has drilled there since 1975
The Galapagos were first settled by Spanish colonists in 1492; Darwin arrived 200 years after the first human settlement and found that animals had already become domesticated
questions.answer: The Galapagos Islands (Ecuador, 1,000 km west of the South American mainland) are a volcanic archipelago known for extreme species endemism — animals that evolved in isolation found nowhere else. Darwin's 1835 visit and observation of distinct finch species per island contributed to his theory of natural selection. Today the islands host giant tortoises, marine iguanas (the only ocean-swimming lizard), blue-footed boobies, and flightless cormorants
questions.explanation: Galapagos Islands: 19 main islands, 97% designated national park. Darwin arrived 1835 on HMS Beagle; observed that mockingbirds and tortoises differed between islands. It was not actually the finches that first caught his attention — the tortoises and mockingbirds did. He collected finch specimens without careful island labeling; it was ornithologist John Gould who identified 13 distinct species from Darwin's collection, prompting Darwin to reconsider. The Galapagos marine iguana — the world's only ocean-swimming lizard — evolved from a land iguana ancestor that arrived from South America and adapted to forage underwater for algae. Current threats: invasive species (cats, rats, goats), climate change (El Niño events devastate marine food chains), tourism pressure.
questions.memory_hook: Darwin's observations in the Galapagos didn't immediately trigger his theory of evolution — he misidentified several of the finches during the voyage and didn't carefully track which island he'd collected them from. It was back in England, when ornithologist John Gould told him he had 13 distinct but related species from one archipelago, that Darwin began reconsidering. The famous 'Darwin's finches' that inspired evolution were specimens Darwin hadn't properly catalogued. The theory of natural selection — one of the most important ideas in science — emerged partly from Darwin's own sloppy field notes. Luck and a good ornithologist in London mattered as much as the Galapagos.

questions.question: What is the significance of Greenland's ice sheet to global sea level?
options: Greenland's ice sheet is too small to affect global sea level significantly; Antarctic ice is the only relevant factor

Greenland's ice sheet (1.71 million km²) contains enough ice that its complete melting would raise global sea levels by ~7 meters. It is the world's second-largest ice sheet after Antarctica. Since the 1990s, Greenland has been losing mass at an accelerating rate due to warming. Greenland is geopolitically significant as an autonomous Danish territory with strategic location between the US and Russia, and holds substantial mineral and oil resources
Greenland's ice sheet is stable and expanding; satellite data shows net ice gain since 1980
Greenland's ice sheet is entirely below sea level and therefore has no impact on global sea level — it is contained by the surrounding bedrock
questions.answer: Greenland's ice sheet (1.71 million km²) contains enough ice that its complete melting would raise global sea levels by ~7 meters. It is the world's second-largest ice sheet after Antarctica. Since the 1990s, Greenland has been losing mass at an accelerating rate due to warming. Greenland is geopolitically significant as an autonomous Danish territory with strategic location between the US and Russia, and holds substantial mineral and oil resources
questions.explanation: Greenland's ice sheet: 1.71 million km², average thickness 2.1 km, maximum 3.4 km. Sea level potential: ~7.2 m if entirely melted. Current loss rate: ~280 billion tons/year (accelerating since 1990s). The bedrock under Greenland is below sea level in places — if the ice melts, some areas would be below sea level for thousands of years until bedrock rebounded. US has expressed interest in purchasing Greenland since 1867 (Secretary of State Seward) and again in 2019 (Trump). Denmark called it 'absurd.' Strategic factors: Northwest Passage opening with Arctic warming, mineral resources (rare earth elements, uranium, oil), proximity to potential Russia-NATO conflict zones.
questions.memory_hook: Greenland's ice sheet contains enough ice to raise global sea levels by 7 meters if entirely melted. Seven meters would inundate most of the world's major coastal cities: Miami, Shanghai, Mumbai, Amsterdam, New York, Tokyo, Bangkok. Even a 1-meter rise — achievable within a century — would affect hundreds of millions of people in low-lying coastal areas. Greenland is losing 280 billion tons of ice per year, accelerating. The US has tried to buy Greenland twice — in 1867 and 2019. Denmark both times said it's not for sale. The strategic and environmental significance of this autonomous Danish island is growing.

questions.question: What is the role of enzymes in biological chemistry?
options: Enzymes are consumed in chemical reactions; they must be continuously synthesized by the body

Enzymes are biological catalysts — proteins that speed up chemical reactions by lowering activation energy, without being consumed in the reaction. Each enzyme is highly specific (lock-and-key or induced-fit model) for its substrate. Enzymes regulate metabolism, DNA replication, digestion, immune responses, and virtually every biological process. Temperature and pH affect enzyme activity; extremes denature them. Many drugs work by inhibiting specific enzymes (statins inhibit cholesterol-synthesis enzymes; aspirin inhibits COX enzymes involved in inflammation)
Enzymes are sugars that dissolve reactants; they are found only in plant cells and are consumed during photosynthesis
Enzymes work at any temperature and pH; extremes improve their efficiency by increasing molecular collisions
questions.answer: Enzymes are biological catalysts — proteins that speed up chemical reactions by lowering activation energy, without being consumed in the reaction. Each enzyme is highly specific (lock-and-key or induced-fit model) for its substrate. Enzymes regulate metabolism, DNA replication, digestion, immune responses, and virtually every biological process. Temperature and pH affect enzyme activity; extremes denature them. Many drugs work by inhibiting specific enzymes (statins inhibit cholesterol-synthesis enzymes; aspirin inhibits COX enzymes involved in inflammation)
questions.explanation: Enzyme mechanism: the active site binds the substrate, forms an enzyme-substrate complex, lowers the activation energy needed for the reaction, releases products, and is regenerated. Rate: enzymes can catalyze millions of reactions per second. Examples: amylase (saliva, breaks starch), pepsin (stomach, breaks protein at pH 2), DNA polymerase (replicates DNA), restriction enzymes (cut DNA at specific sequences — used in genetic engineering). Drug targets: statins (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors) lower cholesterol; ACE inhibitors lower blood pressure; protease inhibitors (HIV treatment) block viral enzymes. Industrial: enzymes in detergents break down protein stains at cold water temperatures.
questions.memory_hook: Restriction enzymes are bacterial proteins that cut DNA at specific sequences — bacteria use them as an immune defense against viral DNA. Scientists co-opted them as molecular scissors for genetic engineering. Without restriction enzymes, genetic engineering would be nearly impossible. Similarly, PCR (the COVID test) relies on a heat-stable enzyme (Taq polymerase) from bacteria living in Yellowstone hot springs. Extremophile bacteria — evolved to survive in boiling hot springs — gave us the enzyme that enables modern medical diagnostics. The biotechnology industry is built on enzymes discovered in the most unlikely biological contexts.

questions.question: What is quantum entanglement and why did Einstein call it 'spooky action at a distance'?
options: Quantum entanglement is a hypothetical phenomenon that has never been experimentally observed

Quantum entanglement occurs when two particles interact so that the quantum state of each cannot be described independently. Measuring one particle instantly determines the correlated state of the other, regardless of distance. Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen (1935 EPR paper) argued this violated relativity's prohibition on faster-than-light causation ('spooky action at a distance'). Bell's theorem (1964) and subsequent experiments (Aspect et al., 1982) proved quantum entanglement is real. It underlies quantum computing and quantum cryptography
Quantum entanglement allows faster-than-light communication; military applications have been operational since 2010
Quantum entanglement was disproven by the 2022 Nobel laureates; the EPR correlations are now explained by hidden classical variables
questions.answer: Quantum entanglement occurs when two particles interact so that the quantum state of each cannot be described independently. Measuring one particle instantly determines the correlated state of the other, regardless of distance. Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen (1935 EPR paper) argued this violated relativity's prohibition on faster-than-light causation ('spooky action at a distance'). Bell's theorem (1964) and subsequent experiments (Aspect et al., 1982) proved quantum entanglement is real. It underlies quantum computing and quantum cryptography
questions.explanation: EPR paradox (1935): Einstein, Podolsky, Rosen argued that entanglement implied either superluminal causation or 'hidden variables' (pre-existing values we just haven't measured). Bell's theorem (1964) derived a mathematical inequality that hidden-variable theories must obey but quantum mechanics violates. Aspect's experiments (1982) measured entangled photons, found quantum mechanics' predictions correct — no hidden variables. 2022 Nobel Prize (Aspect, Clauser, Zeilinger) awarded for these Bell-inequality experiments. Entanglement cannot send information faster than light (measuring one particle doesn't let you choose what it measures as — the result is random). But it enables quantum cryptography (any eavesdropping disturbs the entanglement) and quantum computing.
questions.memory_hook: Einstein was wrong about quantum entanglement. He spent the last 20 years of his life arguing that quantum mechanics must be incomplete — that there must be 'hidden variables' determining outcomes that appear random. He called entanglement 'spooky action at a distance' and regarded it as proof that quantum mechanics was not a complete theory. John Bell devised a mathematical test in 1964 that could determine whether hidden variables existed. Alain Aspect ran the experiment in 1982. The results: quantum mechanics was right, Einstein was wrong, and the universe genuinely is as strange as Bohr had claimed. The 2022 Nobel Prize honored the experiments that proved Einstein wrong about quantum reality.
